name: Supermassive black-hole binary GW background
summary: Mergers of supermassive black-hole binaries (M ~ 10^8-10^10 M_sun) formed during galaxy mergers radiate gravitational waves over Myr-Gyr inspiral times. The cosmological population produces a stochastic GW background at nanohertz frequencies with characteristic strain h_c(f) ~ A f^-2/3 and amplitude A ~ 1-3e-15 at f=1/yr - precisely matching the NANOGrav 15-yr signal. This is the canonical (astrophysical) explanation for the PTA detection.

addresses_puzzles.note: Strain amplitude 1-3e-15 at 1/yr and f^-2/3 spectral index match NANOGrav 15-yr posterior. Anisotropy measurements (currently insufficient) and individual-source resolution (with longer time series) would confirm SMBHB origin definitively. Major remaining uncertainty: efficient binary hardening past parsec scales ('final-parsec problem').

touches_benchmarks.note: Predicted A ~ 1-3e-15 (depending on SMBHB merger-rate density and final-parsec efficiency) matches NANOGrav 2.4+0.7-0.6 e-15.

introduces.note: No new physics; uses GR + standard galaxy-merger / SMBHB dynamics. Two effective parameters (merger rate, hardening efficiency) are astrophysical inputs.

history: Begelman, Blandford, Rees (1980), 'Massive black hole binaries in active galactic nuclei', Nature 287, 307 - foundational. Modern population synthesis: Sesana, Vecchio, Colacino (2008) systematic calculation. PTA-detection-driven refinement: Burke-Spolaor et al. (2019), 'The astrophysics of nanohertz gravitational waves', A&A Rev. 27, 5, arXiv:1811.08826. Identified as best-fit source by NANOGrav, EPTA, PPTA 2023.

references.citation: Begelman, Blandford, Rees, 'Massive black hole binaries in active galactic nuclei', Nature 287, 307 (1980)
Burke-Spolaor et al., 'The astrophysics of nanohertz gravitational waves', Astron. Astrophys. Rev. 27, 5 (2019)
